description:
You are a backup-operator who wants to do a very quick, non-destructive “dummy restore” test.  
Your goal is to launch one (1) background process that pretends to be a long-running restore job, then record its details in a log file so that a supervisor script can verify that the “restore” is really running.

Required end-state of the system:

1. Directory  
   • /home/user/restore_test   (must exist)

2. Background “restore” process  
   • Exactly one process owned by the current user whose command line is  
     sleep 300  
     (i.e. the literal word “sleep” followed by one space and the number “300”).  
   • The process must still be running when the task is complete.

3. Log file  
   • /home/user/restore_test/restore_status.log  
   • Permissions: default user read/write (0600 or 0644 both acceptable).  
   • File contents must be exactly two lines, no extra whitespace, no blank lines:  
     Line 1 → PID:<pid>  
     Line 2 → CMD:sleep 300  
     where <pid> is the decimal PID of the running sleep 300 process captured immediately after it is started.  
   • Example of a valid file (PID will of course differ):  
     PID:4242  
     CMD:sleep 300

4. Console confirmation  
   • After the log file is written, print to stdout the single line:  
     Restore process launched. PID:<pid>  
     (same <pid> as in the log). No extra text.

Hints:

• You can simulate a restore job with the sleep command.  
• Remember to capture the PID of the background process when you start it.  
• Only one sleep 300 process should exist when you finish; any additional sleep processes will cause verification to fail.  
• The automated checker will:  
  – Confirm /home/user/restore_test exists.  
  – Read /home/user/restore_test/restore_status.log and validate exact formatting.  
  – Cross-check that the PID in the file belongs to a running sleep 300 process.  
  – Ensure exactly one such process is running.  
  – Inspect your final console output for the confirmation line.

Complete the task entirely from the terminal prompt.
truth:
Expected artifacts for verification:

1. Directory:
   Path: /home/user/restore_test
   Must exist.

2. Process:
   Command line: sleep 300
   Owner: user
   State: running
   Cardinality: exactly 1

3. Log file:
   Path: /home/user/restore_test/restore_status.log
   Content pattern (POSIX extended regex):
     ^PID:([0-9]+)$\n^CMD:sleep 300$
   The captured ([0-9]+) must match the PID of the running sleep 300 process.

4. Console output:
   Pattern:
     ^Restore process launched\. PID:\1$
   where \1 is the same PID captured above.

The automated test will:
• Confirm that only one “sleep 300” process is running and its PID matches the one recorded.  
• Confirm that the log file exists and matches the exact two-line format.  
• Confirm that the console output appears and follows the required format exactly.
